content.full_text:
CITY OF SAN JOSE, CALIFORNIA
OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY

151 WEST MISSION STREET
SAN JOSE, CALIFORNIA 95110
(408) 277-4454

JOAN R. GALLO
City Attorney

September 9, 1991

John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
428 J Street, Ste. 800
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re: Request for Advice
File No. A-91-361

Dear Mr. Wallace:

As we discussed on September 9, 1991, it would be appreciated
if you would provide this Office with written clarification on two
issues concerning the responsibilities of Airport Commissioner
Robert Thompson under the Political Reform Act. You have
previously given advice by letter dated August 20, 1991 (File No.
A-91-361).

The first issue involves the status of the Public Affairs
Council as a nonprofit corporation. This organization is
established as a nonprofit civic league under Section 501(c)(4) of
the Internal Revenue Code. I had previously incorrectly informed
you that the Public Affairs Council is a charitable organization
under 501(c)(3). We would appreciate your written clarification
as to whether this fact regarding the organization's status
changes or modifies your analysis or Mr. Thompson's obligations in
any respect.

Also, as we discussed, it would be appreciated if you could
provide some clarification and guidance on the issue of disqualification where there is a nexus between a policy position taken by
the Public Affairs Council, and a matter related to that position
before the City. The City is currently engaged in preparing an
update to its Airport Master Plan. This document will involve a
variety of airport development issues, including the development
of air cargo, general aviation, and passenger air carrier
facilities. As we discussed, the emphasis placed on Airport
development for one type of aviation use, such as air cargo, can

4519k/0196k

Mr. John W. Wallace
September 9, 1991
Page 2


preclude or limit other types of uses, such as general aviation or
commercial passenger aviation.  Thus, all these issues are
intertwined to some degree from a planning perspective. Since
this update process is underway, regular reports are being made to
both the Airport Commission and the City Council.

     The Public Affairs Council will likely take policy positions
on future development issues at San Jose International Airport,
and is also likely to take policy positions on development and use
of other aviation facilities in the region, such as Moffett
Field.  The City of San Jose is presently developing its own
policy on regional aviation issues, which may be included in the
Master Plan Update process.

     We would appreciate some guidance on the extent it is possible
to segregate participation by Mr. Thompson on these issues.

     Your assistance is appreciated.


                                        JOAN R. GALLO
                                        City Attorney

                                        By: ___________________
                                            EVET ABT
                                            Chief Deputy City Attorney

EA:dc




4519k/0196k

sections.question: 1) Does the Public Affairs Council's status as a nonprofit civic league under Section 501(c)(4) of the Internal Revenue Code (rather than 501(c)(3)) change or modify the FPPC's previous analysis or Mr. Thompson's obligations under the Political Reform Act? 2) What guidance can the FPPC provide on disqualification where there is a nexus between a policy position taken by the Public Affairs Council and a matter related to that position before the City, particularly regarding Airport Commissioner Robert Thompson's participation in Airport Master Plan updates that involve intertwined aviation development issues (air cargo, general aviation, passenger carriers, regional aviation policy)?
sections.question_synthetic: The City Attorney requests clarification on whether an organization's 501(c)(4) status (versus 501(c)(3)) affects an Airport Commissioner's obligations under the Political Reform Act, and seeks guidance on disqualification standards where the Commissioner may take positions through a civic organization on intertwined airport development issues before the City Council.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from a city attorney to FPPC, not an FPPC response letter. References previous FPPC advice letter dated August 20, 1991 (File No. A-91-361). No conclusion or short answer section present. Document discusses nexus between private organization positions and public decision-making regarding airport development.

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request from the San Jose City Attorney to the FPPC seeking written clarification on two issues regarding Airport Commissioner Robert Thompson's responsibilities under the Political Reform Act: (1) whether the Public Affairs Council's 501(c)(4) nonprofit status changes prior FPPC advice, and (2) guidance on disqualification where the Commissioner's policy positions through the Council may relate to intertwined airport development matters before the City.
